blocks.title: Overview
blocks.text:
This final session is not a conclusion — it is a launch. Everything that has come before in fifteen sessions of self-discovery, community engagement, strategic planning, systems analysis, mindset transformation, and resource networking has been moving toward this moment: the presentation, reception of feedback, and accountable commitment to a Breakthrough Plan that is strategic (focused on high-leverage change), significant (courageous and ministry-trajectory-altering), and sustainable (designed to last through accountability structures and built-in resilience). Breakthrough is 'strategic, significant, and sustainable change in the way you live and work.' Breakthrough Leadership is implementing a strategy to empower others for ministry with urban youth. These definitions, first introduced in Session 8, now have fifteen sessions of learning, reflection, and relationship behind them.

The session is structured in three movements. The first is the Breakthrough Plan presentations — each participant delivers a 15-minute summary of their Personal and Ministry Learning Journals, Focus Statements, and Breakthrough Plans, followed by 15 minutes of cohort feedback using the Breakthrough Plan Feedback Form. The feedback form invites peers to assess which Core Values and Breakthrough Skills are evident in the plan, to suggest strategies for overcoming identified barriers, to evaluate the plan's empowerment orientation, and to identify potential synergies with their own Breakthrough Plans. This peer-feedback structure embodies all five Core Values simultaneously: Accountability (honest feedback given and received), Balance (each presenter gets equal time), Interdependence (the cohort as learning community), Empowerment (building each other's capacity to lead), and Leverage (identifying collaborative opportunities across city group plans).

The second movement introduces Collaborative Leadership — the capstone competency that integrates all the prior learning into the ongoing posture of the graduate. Drawing on the six skills identified in the DVULI curriculum (Self-Reflection, Building Trust, Developing People, Assessing the Environment, Creating Clarity and Vision, Sharing Power and Influence) and on Nehemiah as the definitive biblical model of the collaborative leader, participants examine how their DVULI journey has developed each skill and where continued growth is needed. The third movement is a forward-looking exploration of how the city group will continue working together: through ongoing learning circles, collaborative ministry projects, shared resource development, and the accountability relationships that will sustain each person's Breakthrough Plan implementation in the months ahead. The session closes with the DVULI conviction that has been its north star from Session 1: the unique work God has called and equipped each leader to do, in the place He has designed for them, for the purposes He most needs accomplished in the world.
blocks.html:
Session 16

Accelerating Change: Your Breakthrough Plan and the Beginning of What Comes Next

This is not a conclusion — it is a launch. Everything DVULI has built now moves forward through you.

Picture this: It is the morning of Local Workshop 5. The room holds the same eleven people who sat together nervously on the first night of this program fifteen months ago, introducing themselves, unsure what DVULI would ask of them. But something is different now. The nervousness that fills the room today is not the nervousness of strangers — it is the holy, trembling anticipation of people who have done real work on themselves, on their ministries, and on each other. Each person holds a document. Not a homework assignment. Not a workbook exercise. A Breakthrough Plan — their own carefully written account of who they are, what God has called them to build, and precisely how they intend to build it.

One by one they will stand before the cohort. They will be seen — perhaps more fully than at any previous moment in the program. They will receive honest, loving feedback from peers who now know them well enough to tell the truth. And in the hours that follow the presentations, they will commit — publicly, accountably, and with the community of practice (Wenger, 1998) that has sustained them through this journey — to do the specific, courageous, trajectory-altering work to which God has called them. This session is not the end of DVULI. It is the moment DVULI has been preparing them for.

The Arc of the Journey

Fifteen sessions of intentional learning have been building toward this room, this morning, this moment. Session 1 introduced the Personal Assessment Model — the hexagonal framework of Calling, Character, Commitment to Change, Authenticity, Responsiveness, and Teachability — as a diagnostic for understanding one's divine design. Every subsequent workshop and national conference has added a layer of insight, skill, and relational depth. Community Youth Development challenged the cohort to see their neighborhoods through an asset lens rather than a deficit lens. Scenario Planning trained them to imagine multiple futures and act with strategic flexibility. Systems Thinking revealed the hidden structures beneath the surface events of ministry life. Resource Networking equipped them to mobilize people, goods, and services for Kingdom purposes. And through every session, the five Core Values — Accountability, Balance, Interdependence, Empowerment, and Leverage — have served as the ethical and relational spine of all of it.

The Breakthrough Plan that each participant brings to Session 16 is the integrating artifact of that entire journey. According to the DVULI Breakthrough Handbook, Breakthrough is defined as "strategic, significant, and sustainable change in the way you live and work," and Breakthrough Leadership as "implementing a strategy to empower others for ministry with urban youth." These definitions, first introduced at the First National Conference's capstone session on Leverage and Breakthrough, now carry the full weight of fifteen months of learning, reflection, relationship, and courageous self-examination behind them. The plan is not a theoretical document. It is a covenant with a specific future that God has called each leader to help create.

Research on the transfer of training illuminates why Session 16 matters so much. Baldwin and Ford's (1988) landmark review of the training transfer literature found that fewer than ten percent of training investment typically transfers to sustained job performance without deliberate environmental reinforcement, supervisor support, and peer accountability. DVULI's design counters this with extraordinary intentionality: the Breakthrough Plan presentation creates a moment of public commitment, the cohort feedback structures ongoing peer accountability, and the alumni community of practice provides the relational environment in which the plan can actually live and breathe over time.

Think About It

Before reading on, pause and consider: Of the fifteen months you have invested in this program, which single insight or experience do you expect to be most central to your Breakthrough Plan? What made it so formative — and what structural conditions will you need to put in place to ensure it does not fade?

Movement One: Breakthrough Plan Presentations and Peer Feedback

The Structure of Presentation

Session 16 is structured in three movements. The first — and most emotionally and spiritually significant — is the Breakthrough Plan presentation sequence. Each participant presents a fifteen-minute oral summary of their Personal and Ministry Learning Journals, Focus Statements, and Breakthrough Plans, followed by fifteen minutes of structured cohort feedback using the DVULI Breakthrough Plan Feedback Form. The thirty-minute cycle per presenter is carefully designed: the time constraint forces presenters to distill their learning to its essential core, and the feedback structure channels the cohort's responses toward specific, constructive, and forward-looking input.

The presentation itself invites each leader to do something that is both intellectually demanding and personally vulnerable: to narrate their own transformation. Effective presenters do not simply read their plan. They tell the story of what they discovered about themselves, what shifted in their understanding of their ministry, and what specific, high-leverage changes they are now committed to implementing. They are practicing what Goleman, Boyatzis, and McKee (2013) describe as the foundational competency of resonant leadership — the capacity for self-awareness and self-disclosure that creates genuine connection with others and invites authentic collaboration. The presentation is an act of leadership, not just a reporting event.

The Feedback Form as a Learning Instrument

The Breakthrough Plan Feedback Form is a sophisticated pedagogical tool that structures cohort responses along five dimensions. Peers are invited to assess which of the five Core Values are clearly evident in the presenter's plan; which Breakthrough Skills — Community Youth Development, Scenario Planning, Systems Thinking, Resource Networking, and Collaboration — are most prominently reflected in the strategy; and to evaluate the plan's orientation toward empowering others for ministry with urban youth rather than consolidating ministry activity around the leader themselves.

Two additional dimensions complete the form: peers suggest specific strategies for overcoming the presenter's identified barriers, and they identify potential synergies between the presenter's plan and their own — pointing toward the collaborative ministry possibilities that will animate the third movement of this session. This feedback architecture is grounded in what Boud, Cohen, and Sampson (2001) describe as the core advantage of structured peer learning: when learners move from passive recipients to active co-constructors of each other's knowledge, both the quality of learning and the depth of accountability increase dramatically.

Perhaps most importantly, the feedback form embodies all five Core Values simultaneously. The honest appraisal of a peer's plan is an act of Accountability. The equal time allocation for each presenter reflects Balance. The cohort functioning as a learning community is the very definition of Interdependence. Building each participant's capacity to lead is Empowerment. And identifying synergies across plans for potential collaboration is the essence of Leverage. The feedback session is not an administrative requirement appended to the presentations — it is a live demonstration of everything DVULI has been teaching.

Breakthrough Plan Peer Feedback Tool

Use this tool to record your structured feedback as a cohort reviewer. Complete one form per presenter. When all reviewers have submitted, view the aggregated radar summary.

Movement Two: Collaborative Leadership — The Capstone Competency

Six Skills That Integrate Everything

The second movement of Session 16 introduces Collaborative Leadership — defined in the DVULI curriculum as "the capacity to bring peers together, and enable them to look beyond their own talent and resources to accomplish a shared goal." The collaborative leader inspires commitment and action in others and uses their influence to keep diverse stakeholders engaged with a shared vision. This is the capstone competency of the DVULI program, because it is only through collaborative leadership that Breakthrough Plans achieve their full potential impact.

The DVULI curriculum identifies six specific skills of collaborative leadership, drawn from the Turning Point Collaborative Leadership framework. Together, these skills map almost perfectly onto the arc of learning across all fifteen prior sessions:

  • Self-Reflection — Understanding one's own values and ensuring behaviors are congruent with them. This was the work of Session 1 (Personal Assessment), the First National Conference (Core Values), and the Anatomy of Peace (Arbinger) sessions.
  • Building Trust — Creating the relational conditions in which diverse stakeholders can share ideas honestly and remain committed through difficulty. This was developed through the communication tools of Local Workshop 1, the mentoring relationships, and the cohort's group dialogues.
  • Developing People — The Empowerment Core Value in action: mentoring, coaching, and releasing others into leadership. Local Workshop 2's Community Youth Development and the Breakthrough Plan's empowerment orientation both live here.
  • Assessing the Environment — Reading the systems, scenarios, and stakeholders accurately enough to identify common ground and plan for obstacles. Local Workshop 3 (Scenario Planning), the Second National Conference (Systems Thinking), and the community asset mapping exercises all built this capacity.
  • Creating Clarity and Vision — Articulating a compelling, shared vision that transcends individual self-interest. The Breakthrough Plan presentations themselves are an exercise in this skill.
  • Sharing Power and Influence — Structuring decision-making processes that draw on the expertise and ownership of everyone at the table, not just the designated leader. Resource Networking and the 5 Easy Pieces game both illustrated this principle experientially.

Kouzes and Posner (2017) argue across decades of research that "Enable Others to Act" — the practice most closely aligned with Sharing Power and Influence — is consistently among the most impactful of the five exemplary leadership practices, yet also among the most counterintuitive for leaders who have been rewarded throughout their careers for individual expertise and decisive action. DVULI's curriculum recognizes this tension and engages it directly through every session. The leader who graduates from DVULI should be measurably more comfortable with distributed leadership, shared authority, and collective ownership of outcomes than the leader who enrolled fifteen months ago.

Think About It

Of the six collaborative leadership skills, which feels most natural to you after this program — and which still represents your growing edge? What specific experience in DVULI most challenged your default approach to that skill? How does your Breakthrough Plan address it?

Collaborative Leadership Six-Skills Growth Tracker

Rate yourself on each of the six Collaborative Leadership skills at three time points. The canvas below will render your full growth arc. Click any skill name to see its DVULI connections and next-action prompt.

Nehemiah as the Definitive Biblical Model

The DVULI curriculum has used Nehemiah as a leadership case study across multiple sessions, but Session 16 positions him as the definitive biblical model of collaborative leadership — the leader who embodies all six skills in a single, sustained ministry project. Gary Smith's (2010) commentary on Ezra-Nehemiah traces the full arc of Nehemiah's leadership method with exegetical precision, and the pattern is unmistakable.

Nehemiah's environmental assessment (Neh. 2:11–16) — the famous nighttime circuit of Jerusalem's broken walls, conducted privately before he disclosed his vision to a single stakeholder — is a masterclass in reading the system before attempting to change it. His transparent communication of vision (Neh. 2:17–18), in which he first names the problem honestly ("You see the trouble we are in"), then articulates a divinely-sourced possibility, and only then invites shared commitment ("Let us rebuild"), maps precisely onto the "Creating Clarity and Vision" skill of collaborative leadership. His coalition-building across tribal, occupational, and gender lines (Neh. 3), which includes at least one group of daughters working alongside their father (Neh. 3:12), is a living illustration of sharing power and influence across every available boundary.

Heifetz and Linsky (2002) draw on Nehemiah extensively in their framework for adaptive leadership, noting that the wall-rebuilding project is not a technical problem — the techniques of masonry were well known — but an adaptive challenge requiring the community of Jerusalem to change how they understood themselves, their capacity for self-governance, and their relationship to the broken covenant with God. Nehemiah's response to Sanballat and Tobiah's opposition (Neh. 4, 6) demonstrates what Heifetz and Linsky call "holding steady under pressure" — refusing to be drawn off the balcony and into the fray at a level that would disrupt the larger process. The accountability structures for completion (Neh. 7), including the registration and reestablishment of community life after the wall was finished, model the sustainability dimension of Breakthrough that the DVULI curriculum insists upon.

Movement Three: The City Group Forward

Communities of Practice Beyond the Program

The third movement of Session 16 turns the cohort's gaze forward — not to a conclusion, but to a continuation. Etienne Wenger's (1998) foundational theory of communities of practice provides the conceptual architecture for understanding why this matters. Wenger argues that learning is not primarily an individual cognitive event but a fundamentally social one: it is "situated in participation within communities" where "shared practice, mutual engagement, and joint enterprise create sustained professional identity and knowledge development." The DVULI city group, over fifteen months, has become precisely such a community. The question is what kind of intentionality is required to sustain it after the formal program ends.

The DVULI curriculum identifies four primary modalities through which the city group can continue to function as a productive community of practice. First, ongoing learning circles — regular gatherings, meeting every six to eight weeks, in which group members share what they are encountering in their Breakthrough Plan implementation, raise new challenges for group dialogue, and continue the practices of giving and receiving honest feedback that have characterized the program. Pedler and Abbott (2013) describe this as the core mechanism of action learning sets: "working on real problems with real stakes" in "small groups that provide challenge and support simultaneously." The learning circle is not a social gathering with a spiritual veneer — it is a structured accountability and growth environment.

Second, collaborative ministry projects — specific initiatives that emerge from the synergies identified in the Breakthrough Plan feedback process. The Breakthrough Plan Feedback Form explicitly asks each reviewer to identify where their own plan intersects with the presenter's. When multiple participants in a single city group are working on related aspects of youth development — one focused on leadership pipeline development, another on community asset mapping, a third on empowerment-oriented discipleship — the natural collaboration potential is significant. DVULI's collaboration grants program exists precisely to resource these city-group ministry partnerships.

Third, shared resource development — leveraging the collective expertise, networks, and platform of the entire cohort to access resources that no single ministry could attract alone. Local Workshop 4's Resource Networking content equipped participants with the tools to articulate their "Why" powerfully and to identify partners across multiple resource circles. The city group, functioning as a unified community of practice, can now bring these skills to bear on behalf of all its members' Breakthrough Plans collectively.

Fourth — and most fundamental — the accountability relationships that will sustain each person's Breakthrough Plan implementation in the months ahead. Each graduate is expected to identify an accountability partner, check in monthly, and report on both progress and unexpected barriers. This structure embodies the Accountability Core Value at its most specific and practical: "honest introspection, the prayerful search for wisdom, and the development of Accountability relationships characterized by a mutual commitment to seek and tell the truth concerning each other's thoughts and actions" (DVULI First National Conference Workbook).

"If you want to go fast, go alone. If you want to go far, go together." — African proverb, cited in the DVULI Local Workshop 5 Pre-Assignment

The Personal Assessment Model Revisited: Calling, Character, and Commitment to Change

Session 16 closes the loop on the journey that began in Session 1 with the most important act of self-examination DVULI asks of its graduates: a full-arc revisitation of the Personal Assessment Model. The six-dimensional hexagon — Calling, Character, Commitment to Change, Authenticity, Responsiveness, and Teachability — was introduced in Local Workshop 1 as a diagnostic framework. Now, at the program's end, it serves as a mirror in which each graduate can see both the distance they have traveled and the direction that remains ahead.

The DVULI Personal Assessment Model (LW1 Workbook, p. 8) defines these dimensions with precision:

  • Calling: "Faithfully doing the work that God has called and equipped me to do as my contribution to building His Kingdom." This dimension asks whether the graduate's Breakthrough Plan aligns more fully with their sense of divine calling than when they entered the program — and whether that sense of calling has been clarified, deepened, or perhaps redirected by the learning journey.
  • Character: "Consistently living out the values I espouse, applying them to my every action and decision." The fifteen months of Core Values work — and especially the honest feedback received from mentors, cohort members, and City Coordinators — should have produced measurable growth in the congruence between espoused values and lived behavior.
  • Commitment to Change: "Willingly adjusting my thoughts, plans and actions to align them more fully with God's will for my life and ministry." This is perhaps the dimension that the program tests most rigorously. The adaptive challenges Heifetz and Linsky (2002) describe — the genuine losses, the identity disruptions, the necessary letting go of familiar patterns — are precisely the crucible in which Commitment to Change is forged or found wanting.
  • Authenticity (Calling + Character): "Integrating both doing what God calls me to do and being who God has designed me to be." This is the synthesis dimension — the evidence that the internal work and the external work have begun to converge.
  • Responsiveness (Calling + Commitment to Change): "Having the attitude of Christ — being willing to submit my will to God's will, as modeled by Jesus in his responsiveness to people, circumstances, and most importantly, His Father's will."
  • Teachability (Character + Commitment to Change): "Wanting to be transformed through the renewing of my mind — in essence, being a learner rather than a knower." This is the disposition that made fifteen months of stretching, confronting, and transformative learning possible — and it is the disposition that will determine whether the next fifteen months produce further growth or gradual reversion to familiar patterns.

The Divine Design Portrait

The completed hexagon assessment, overlaid with the Session 1 baseline scores and annotated with three reflective paragraphs per dimension — "What shifted?", "What specific experience developed it?", and "What does this growth mean for my Breakthrough Plan?" — is compiled into what DVULI calls the Divine Design Portrait. This one-page artifact is both a graduation document and an accountability anchor: a precise, personal record of the leader that this graduate has become through the DVULI journey, and a baseline against which continued growth can be measured in the years ahead. It is, in the most literal sense, a portrait of divine design: the unique work God has called and equipped this specific person to do, in the place He has designed for them, for the purposes He most needs accomplished in the world (DVULI First National Conference Workbook, p. 44; LW1 Workbook, p. 6).

Personal Assessment Model: Full-Arc Revisited

Enter your Session 1 (beginning) and current end-of-program ratings for all six dimensions. The hexagon will display both polygons, shading growth in green and any decline in amber. Click each dimension name to write your learning reflection.

The North Star: Called, Equipped, Placed, and Purposeful

Session 16 closes with the conviction that has been DVULI's north star from the very first session. The biblical foundation of Local Workshop 1 frames the entire DVULI journey with Paul's words from Galatians 6 and Romans 12: "be transformed by the renewing of your mind... Do not think of yourself more highly than you ought, but rather think of yourself with sober judgment, in accordance with the faith God has distributed to each of you." The journey that began with that honest, sometimes painful, always generative self-examination reaches its culmination not in a certificate or a celebration, but in a commitment: to do the unique work God has called and equipped each leader to do, in the place He has designed for them, for the purposes He most needs accomplished in the world.

That commitment is what the Breakthrough Plan represents at its most fundamental level. It is not a strategic document in the conventional organizational sense. It is a statement of identity, vocation, and accountability — a public covenant that says: I know who I am. I know what I am called to build. I know how I will do it, with whom I will do it, and what I will do when the inevitable barriers arise. I know that I cannot do it alone, and I have built the community of accountability and collaboration that will hold me to it. And I know that the measure of my success is not what I accomplish by myself, but how many others I have equipped, released, and empowered to lead alongside me.

This is Breakthrough Leadership. And it begins today.

Key Takeaways

  • Session 16 is a launch, not a conclusion — the Breakthrough Plan presentations mark the beginning of implementation, not the end of learning.
  • The Peer Feedback Form is a living enactment of all five Core Values simultaneously: Accountability, Balance, Interdependence, Empowerment, and Leverage.
  • Collaborative Leadership — the six-skill capstone competency — integrates every prior session's learning into an ongoing posture of empowering others for ministry.
  • Nehemiah is the definitive biblical model of collaborative leadership: he assessed the environment, built trust, communicated vision transparently, shared power across every available boundary, and built accountability structures to sustain the work.
  • The city group's ongoing community of practice — through learning circles, collaborative projects, shared resource development, and accountability relationships — is the structural mechanism that ensures Breakthrough Plan implementation does not fade within the first year (Baldwin & Ford, 1988).
  • The revisited Personal Assessment Model hexagon, annotated with reflective paragraphs, becomes the Divine Design Portrait: a precise record of the leader this graduate has become, and an anchor for continued growth.
  • Breakthrough is "strategic, significant, and sustainable change in the way you live and work." Breakthrough Leadership is "implementing a strategy to empower others for ministry with urban youth." These definitions now carry fifteen months of lived experience behind them.

Looking Ahead: Life After DVULI

The graduate's journey continues through monthly accountability check-ins, ongoing city group learning circles, collaboration grant applications for joint ministry projects, and the alumni network that connects DVULI graduates across more than twenty-five years of cohorts. The question the program leaves with each graduate is the same one with which it began: "What is the unique work God has called and equipped you to do, in the place He has designed for you, for the purposes He most needs accomplished in the world?" The answer has been coming into focus across fifteen months. Now it is time to live it.

blocks.title: Key Concepts
concepts: Breakthrough Plan presentation and peer feedback: the culminating learning and accountability event
Collaborative Leadership: six skills (Self-Reflection, Trust-Building, Developing People, Assessing Environment, Creating Clarity, Sharing Power) and Nehemiah as exemplar
City group partnership: ongoing learning transfer, ministry collaboration, and the alumni community of practice
The DVULI Personal Assessment Model revisited: Calling, Character, Commitment to Change and how the full program has developed each dimension

items.question: According to the DVULI curriculum, what three qualities define a Breakthrough Plan?
items.correctFeedback: A Breakthrough is defined as 'strategic, significant, and sustainable change in the way you live and work.' Strategic means focused on high-leverage change, significant means courageous and ministry-trajectory-altering, and sustainable means designed to last through accountability structures and built-in resilience.

distractors.text: Spiritual, scalable, and structured
distractors.feedback: While these sound like reasonable qualities of a good plan, they are not the three defining characteristics of Breakthrough as defined in the DVULI curriculum. Students may confuse general planning language with the specific DVULI framework.

distractors.text: Strategic, spiritual, and shared
distractors.feedback: Only 'strategic' is correct here. Students might select this because spiritual formation and shared leadership are important DVULI themes, but the precise definition uses 'significant' and 'sustainable,' not these terms.

distractors.text: Significant, systematic, and servant-oriented
distractors.feedback: Only 'significant' is correct. Students might choose this because systems thinking and servant leadership are emphasized throughout the program, but the official definition pairs 'significant' with 'strategic' and 'sustainable.'

items.question: How is Breakthrough Leadership specifically defined in the DVULI curriculum?
items.correctAnswer: Implementing a strategy to empower others for ministry with urban youth
items.correctFeedback: Breakthrough Leadership is defined as 'implementing a strategy to empower others for ministry with urban youth.' This definition highlights that leadership in the DVULI context is not about personal achievement but about empowering others, and it is specifically oriented toward urban youth ministry.

distractors.text: Developing a personal vision for systemic change in one's community
distractors.feedback: While personal vision and systemic change are themes in DVULI, this definition focuses too narrowly on the individual leader and misses the critical emphasis on empowering others for ministry with urban youth.

distractors.text: Creating strategic, significant, and sustainable change in the way you live and work
distractors.feedback: This is actually the definition of Breakthrough itself, not Breakthrough Leadership. Students may confuse the two, but Breakthrough Leadership specifically emphasizes empowering others for ministry with urban youth.

distractors.text: Leading collaborative efforts that align multiple organizations around shared ministry goals
distractors.feedback: Collaboration is an important DVULI value, but this answer describes one aspect of collaborative leadership rather than the specific curriculum definition of Breakthrough Leadership, which centers on empowering others for urban youth ministry.

items.question: During the Breakthrough Plan presentations, each participant receives 15 minutes of cohort feedback. Which of the following is a task the Breakthrough Plan Feedback Form asks peers to perform?
items.correctAnswer: Identify potential synergies between the presenter's plan and their own Breakthrough Plans
items.correctFeedback: The Feedback Form specifically asks peers to identify potential synergies with their own Breakthrough Plans. This task reflects the Core Value of Leverage — finding collaborative opportunities across city group plans — and embodies the interdependent nature of the DVULI learning community.

distractors.text: Score the presenter's plan on a numerical rubric for each of the six Collaborative Leadership skills
distractors.feedback: Students might assume a formal scoring rubric is used since this is a culminating event, but the Feedback Form focuses on identifying Core Values, Breakthrough Skills, barriers, empowerment orientation, and synergies — not numerical scoring of Collaborative Leadership skills.

distractors.text: Rank the presenter's plan against other cohort members' plans for implementation readiness
distractors.feedback: Comparative ranking would undermine the spirit of mutual empowerment and accountability that the feedback process is designed to foster. The form asks peers to assess and support each plan individually, not to rank plans against one another.

distractors.text: Assign an accountability partner from outside the cohort to monitor plan implementation
distractors.feedback: While accountability is a Core Value, the Feedback Form does not involve assigning external accountability partners. The form focuses on peer assessment of the plan's qualities and on identifying collaborative opportunities within the cohort.

items.question: The peer-feedback structure of the Breakthrough Plan presentations is described as embodying all five DVULI Core Values simultaneously. Which Core Value is demonstrated specifically through the practice of identifying collaborative opportunities across city group plans?
items.correctFeedback: Leverage is demonstrated when peers identify collaborative opportunities across their respective Breakthrough Plans. Leverage in DVULI means finding high-impact connections and multiplying resources — in this case, recognizing where plans can reinforce or complement each other for greater collective impact.

distractors.text: Interdependence
distractors.feedback: Interdependence is embodied in the feedback process through the cohort functioning as a learning community, but the specific act of identifying collaborative opportunities across plans aligns with Leverage, which focuses on multiplying impact through strategic connections.

distractors.text: Empowerment
distractors.feedback: Empowerment is demonstrated in the feedback process through building each other's capacity to lead. While collaborating could empower participants, the specific act of identifying cross-plan synergies is tied to the Core Value of Leverage.

distractors.text: Accountability
distractors.feedback: Accountability is demonstrated through the honest giving and receiving of feedback during the presentation process. Identifying collaborative opportunities, however, aligns more precisely with Leverage, which concerns maximizing impact through strategic partnerships.

items.question: Which biblical figure serves as the definitive model of Collaborative Leadership in the DVULI curriculum?
items.correctFeedback: Nehemiah is presented as the definitive biblical model of the collaborative leader. His story exemplifies all six Collaborative Leadership skills — self-reflection, building trust, developing people, assessing the environment, creating clarity and vision, and sharing power and influence — making him the ideal exemplar for DVULI graduates.

distractors.text: Moses
distractors.feedback: Moses is a prominent biblical leader who delegated authority (e.g., Exodus 18), which could seem relevant to collaborative leadership. However, the DVULI curriculum specifically identifies Nehemiah, not Moses, as the definitive collaborative leadership model.

distractors.text: Paul
distractors.feedback: Paul demonstrated many collaborative qualities in his ministry partnerships, which might lead students to select him. However, the DVULI curriculum specifically designates Nehemiah as the exemplar of Collaborative Leadership.

distractors.text: David
distractors.feedback: David is often studied for his leadership qualities, including his ability to build loyal teams. However, the DVULI curriculum intentionally selects Nehemiah as the definitive collaborative leader, likely because Nehemiah's story uniquely illustrates community mobilization and shared rebuilding.

items.question: The three dimensions of the DVULI Personal Assessment Model revisited in the final session are Calling, Character, and Commitment to Change. How does this model function at the conclusion of the program?
items.correctAnswer: It provides a framework for participants to assess how the full program has developed each dimension of their leadership
items.correctFeedback: The Personal Assessment Model is revisited so participants can reflect on how all fifteen sessions of learning have developed their Calling, Character, and Commitment to Change. It serves as a comprehensive self-assessment framework that connects the entire DVULI journey to measurable personal and leadership growth.

distractors.text: It is used by facilitators to formally evaluate and certify participants for program graduation
distractors.feedback: Students might assume the model serves as a summative evaluation tool for certification, but it is actually a self-assessment framework. Its purpose is reflective — helping participants themselves see their growth across all three dimensions.

distractors.text: It replaces the Breakthrough Plan as the primary deliverable participants present to their cohort
distractors.feedback: The Breakthrough Plan remains the culminating deliverable that participants present. The Personal Assessment Model complements the plan by helping participants reflect on their holistic development, but it does not replace the Breakthrough Plan presentation.

distractors.text: It introduces new developmental goals that participants must add to their Breakthrough Plans before graduation
distractors.feedback: The revisiting of the model is not about adding new requirements to the Breakthrough Plan. Rather, it is a reflective exercise that helps participants recognize how their Calling, Character, and Commitment to Change have matured through the program experience.

items.question: Which of the following is one of the six Collaborative Leadership skills identified in the DVULI curriculum?
items.correctFeedback: Sharing Power and Influence is one of the six Collaborative Leadership skills, alongside Self-Reflection, Building Trust, Developing People, Assessing the Environment, and Creating Clarity and Vision. This skill reflects the DVULI emphasis on empowerment and distributed leadership rather than hierarchical control.

distractors.text: Strategic Resource Acquisition
distractors.feedback: While resource development is a theme in DVULI (particularly around networking and leveraging assets), Strategic Resource Acquisition is not one of the six named Collaborative Leadership skills. Students may confuse broader program themes with this specific competency framework.

distractors.text: Conflict Mediation and Resolution
distractors.feedback: Conflict resolution is an important leadership skill, and it may arise naturally in collaborative work. However, it is not named as one of the six Collaborative Leadership skills in the DVULI curriculum. The actual skills focus on trust, reflection, development, environment, clarity, and shared power.

distractors.text: Organizational Systems Design
distractors.feedback: Systems analysis is a topic covered in earlier DVULI sessions, which might lead students to select this. However, the six Collaborative Leadership skills focus on relational and visionary capacities, not on organizational systems design as a named competency.

items.question: The city group partnership is described as having several ongoing functions after program completion. Which of the following best represents the full scope of the city group's post-program role?
items.correctAnswer: Ongoing learning circles, collaborative ministry projects, shared resource development, and accountability relationships
items.correctFeedback: The city group continues through multiple interconnected functions: learning circles for continued education, collaborative ministry projects for shared impact, shared resource development for mutual benefit, and accountability relationships that sustain each person's Breakthrough Plan implementation. This comprehensive approach ensures learning transfer extends well beyond the program.

distractors.text: Annual reunions, newsletter updates, and optional mentoring relationships structured through a formalized alumni network coordinator
distractors.feedback: This describes a passive alumni network rather than the active, ongoing partnership the DVULI envisions. The city group model is far more engaged and structured, involving regular learning, collaboration, resource sharing, and mutual accountability.

distractors.text: Peer evaluation of Breakthrough Plan progress, with reports submitted to DVULI national leadership for accreditation purposes
distractors.feedback: The city group's accountability is rooted in mutual relationships among peers, not in a reporting structure to national leadership. Students might assume a hierarchical accountability model, but DVULI emphasizes peer-based, relational accountability.

distractors.text: Informal social networking with occasional collaborative opportunities as they arise naturally
distractors.feedback: This understates the intentionality of the city group model. DVULI designs post-program engagement to be structured and purposeful — not merely social or occasional — including specific mechanisms like learning circles and collaborative projects.

items.question: Session 16 is described as having three movements. A participant presents a 15-minute summary during the first movement. Which combination of documents does this summary draw from?
items.correctAnswer: Personal and Ministry Learning Journals, Focus Statements, and Breakthrough Plans
items.correctFeedback: The presentation integrates three key documents developed throughout the program: Personal and Ministry Learning Journals (capturing ongoing reflection), Focus Statements (distilling key insights and direction), and Breakthrough Plans (articulating the strategic change initiative). Together, these represent the full arc of each participant's DVULI journey.

distractors.text: Breakthrough Plans, Collaborative Leadership assessments, and DVULI Personal Assessment Models
distractors.feedback: While the Breakthrough Plan is correct, Collaborative Leadership is addressed in the second movement of the session, not the first. The presentation draws from Learning Journals and Focus Statements alongside the Breakthrough Plan.

distractors.text: Personal and Ministry Learning Journals, city group partnership agreements, and resource network maps
distractors.feedback: Although Learning Journals are part of the presentation, city group partnership agreements and resource network maps are not identified as components of the 15-minute summary. The presentation specifically integrates Journals, Focus Statements, and Breakthrough Plans.

distractors.text: Focus Statements, systems analysis reports, and peer feedback summaries from previous sessions
distractors.feedback: Focus Statements are part of the presentation, but systems analysis reports and prior peer feedback summaries are not listed as components. The summary specifically draws from Learning Journals, Focus Statements, and Breakthrough Plans.

items.question: The session closes with a conviction described as DVULI's north star from Session 1. Which statement best captures this conviction?
items.correctAnswer: God has called and equipped each leader for unique work, in the place He has designed for them, for the purposes He most needs accomplished in the world
items.correctFeedback: This conviction — that God has uniquely called, equipped, and placed each leader for specific kingdom purposes — has been the foundational north star of the entire DVULI program from its very first session. Closing with it reinforces that the program is a launch, not a conclusion, sending graduates forward into the specific work God has prepared for them.

distractors.text: Effective urban youth ministry requires collaborative leadership that prioritizes strategic resource leverage across organizational boundaries
distractors.feedback: While collaboration and leverage are important DVULI themes, this statement describes a programmatic principle rather than the deeply personal, theological conviction about each leader's unique calling that serves as the program's north star.

distractors.text: Breakthrough change is only possible when leaders commit to lifelong learning within a community of practice
distractors.feedback: Lifelong learning and community are valued in DVULI, but the north star conviction is specifically about God's unique calling and placement of each individual leader. This answer confuses an important program mechanism with the foundational theological conviction.

distractors.text: The most impactful leaders are those who master all six Collaborative Leadership skills and model them consistently for others
distractors.feedback: Collaborative Leadership is the capstone competency addressed in Session 16, but it is not the foundational conviction that has guided the program from Session 1. The north star is a theological conviction about God's unique purpose for each leader, not a competency framework.

questions.stem: According to the DVULI curriculum, Breakthrough is defined as which type of change in the way you live and work?
options: Strategic, significant, and sustainable change
Rapid, radical, and reversible change
Personal, professional, and public change
Incremental, intentional, and institutional change

feedback.correct: Breakthrough is defined as 'strategic, significant, and sustainable change in the way you live and work.' Each adjective matters: strategic means focused on high-leverage points, significant means courageous and trajectory-altering, and sustainable means designed to last through accountability and resilience structures.
feedback.incorrect: Many participants confuse Breakthrough with generic notions of improvement. The DVULI definition is precise: strategic (focused on high-leverage change), significant (courageous and ministry-trajectory-altering), and sustainable (designed to endure). This definition was first introduced in Session 8 and now carries fifteen sessions of meaning.

variants.stem: Breakthrough Leadership, as defined in the DVULI curriculum, is best described as:
options: Implementing a strategy to empower others for ministry with urban youth
Achieving personal success through disciplined spiritual practices
Building the largest possible ministry organization in your city
Developing a five-year strategic plan for your church

feedback.correct: Breakthrough Leadership is specifically defined as 'implementing a strategy to empower others for ministry with urban youth.' This definition emphasizes both the empowerment orientation and the specific focus on urban youth ministry that distinguishes DVULI's approach.
feedback.incorrect: Breakthrough Leadership is not about personal success or organizational size. It is specifically defined as implementing a strategy to empower others for ministry with urban youth. The empowerment of others — not personal achievement — is at the heart of this definition.

questions.stem: Which of the following is one of the six Collaborative Leadership skills identified in the DVULI curriculum?
options: Sharing Power and Influence
Fundraising and Grant Writing
Conflict Avoidance
Program Evaluation

feedback.correct: Sharing Power and Influence is one of the six Collaborative Leadership skills, alongside Self-Reflection, Building Trust, Developing People, Assessing the Environment, and Creating Clarity and Vision. These skills integrate all prior DVULI learning into an ongoing leadership posture.
feedback.incorrect: The six Collaborative Leadership skills are: Self-Reflection, Building Trust, Developing People, Assessing the Environment, Creating Clarity and Vision, and Sharing Power and Influence. While fundraising and evaluation are useful ministry skills, they are not among the six named collaborative leadership competencies.

variants.stem: The DVULI curriculum identifies six skills of Collaborative Leadership. Which set correctly names three of them?
options: Self-Reflection, Building Trust, and Creating Clarity and Vision
Budget Management, Strategic Planning, and Staff Hiring
Preaching, Counseling, and Community Organizing
Self-Promotion, Delegating Tasks, and Measuring Outcomes

feedback.correct: Self-Reflection, Building Trust, and Creating Clarity and Vision are three of the six Collaborative Leadership skills. The complete list also includes Developing People, Assessing the Environment, and Sharing Power and Influence.
feedback.incorrect: The six Collaborative Leadership skills are specifically named in the DVULI curriculum: Self-Reflection, Building Trust, Developing People, Assessing the Environment, Creating Clarity and Vision, and Sharing Power and Influence. These focus on relational and reflective competencies rather than technical management tasks.

questions.stem: During the Breakthrough Plan presentations, each participant receives peer feedback using the Breakthrough Plan Feedback Form. What does this peer-feedback structure primarily embody?
options: All five Core Values operating simultaneously
Only the Core Value of Accountability
The six Collaborative Leadership skills
The three dimensions of the Personal Assessment Model
justifications: Accountability appears in honest feedback, Balance in equal time, Interdependence in community learning, Empowerment in building capacity, and Leverage in identifying collaborative opportunities across plans
The feedback form asks peers to hold each other accountable, which is the singular purpose of the exercise
The five Core Values only apply to individual reflection, not group activities like peer feedback
All five Core Values operate because they were each introduced in separate sessions and this is the final session

feedback.correct: The peer-feedback structure is a living demonstration of all five Core Values working together: Accountability (honest feedback given and received), Balance (each presenter gets equal time), Interdependence (the cohort functions as a learning community), Empowerment (building each other's capacity to lead), and Leverage (identifying collaborative opportunities across city group plans).
feedback.incorrect: While Accountability is visible in the feedback process, the curriculum explicitly identifies how all five Core Values — Accountability, Balance, Interdependence, Empowerment, and Leverage — are simultaneously embodied in this single activity. Reducing it to one value misses the integrated design.
feedback.wrongReason: Some students correctly identify that all five Core Values are at work but cannot articulate how each specific value manifests in the activity. Knowing the answer is different from understanding the mechanism — each value has a distinct expression in the feedback structure.

variants.stem: The Breakthrough Plan Feedback Form invites peers to assess Core Values and Breakthrough Skills, suggest barrier strategies, evaluate empowerment orientation, and identify synergies. What is the best description of this multi-dimensional feedback design?
options: It simultaneously activates all five Core Values through a single structured activity
It is primarily a quality-control mechanism to ensure plans meet minimum standards
It replaces the need for ongoing city group accountability
It focuses exclusively on identifying weaknesses in each plan
justifications: Each feedback dimension maps to a specific Core Value: honest assessment to Accountability, equal time to Balance, community learning to Interdependence, capacity-building to Empowerment, and cross-plan synergies to Leverage
The form's multiple dimensions exist because different evaluators prefer different formats, not because they map to specific values
All five Core Values are present because the feedback form explicitly lists them as checkboxes for peers to mark
The form activates all five values because any group activity in DVULI automatically embodies every value

feedback.correct: The feedback form's design is intentional: each dimension of feedback corresponds to a Core Value in action. Assessing values and skills is Accountability, equal presentation time is Balance, cohort learning is Interdependence, building capacity is Empowerment, and identifying synergies is Leverage.
feedback.incorrect: The feedback form is not merely a quality-control tool. Its multi-dimensional design is specifically constructed so that the act of giving and receiving feedback becomes a living demonstration of all five Core Values working in concert.
feedback.wrongReason: Students may recognize the connection to all five Core Values but attribute it to a superficial reason (like the form listing them) rather than understanding that each feedback activity structurally embodies a different value.

questions.stem: Evaluate the relationship between these two statements:
questions.assertion: Nehemiah is used as the definitive biblical model of Collaborative Leadership in the DVULI curriculum.
questions.reason: Nehemiah demonstrated all six Collaborative Leadership skills — Self-Reflection, Building Trust, Developing People, Assessing the Environment, Creating Clarity and Vision, and Sharing Power and Influence — in his rebuilding of Jerusalem's walls.

feedback.correct: Both statements are true, and the reason explains the assertion. Nehemiah is chosen as the exemplar of Collaborative Leadership precisely because his story in Scripture demonstrates all six skills: he reflected on Israel's condition, built trust with the king and the people, developed workers, assessed the broken walls, cast a clear vision, and shared power with local leaders to accomplish the work.
feedback.incorrect: Students sometimes think Nehemiah is chosen simply because he is a well-known leader or because he accomplished a large project. The specific reason he serves as the definitive model is that his leadership demonstrably exhibits each of the six named Collaborative Leadership skills, making the connection between assertion and reason causal, not merely coincidental.

variants.assertion: The Breakthrough Plan presentation session is structured as a launch rather than a conclusion.
variants.reason: Participants present their plans and receive peer feedback to refine and commit to implementation that will continue long after the session ends.

feedback.correct: Both statements are true, and the reason explains the assertion. The session is framed as a launch because the presentations and feedback are designed not to wrap up the program but to propel participants into accountable implementation. The peer feedback, city group partnerships, and alumni community all point forward.
feedback.incorrect: Some students may think the 'launch' framing is merely motivational language. It is structural: the entire session — presentations, feedback forms, city group planning — is designed to initiate ongoing action rather than celebrate completion. The reason accurately explains why it is a launch.

questions.stem: The third movement of Session 16 focuses on how city groups will continue working together after the program. Which of the following is NOT identified as a component of this ongoing partnership?
options: Competitive grant applications between city group members
Ongoing learning circles
Collaborative ministry projects
Shared resource development

feedback.correct: Competitive grant applications are not part of the city group partnership model. The ongoing partnership includes learning circles, collaborative ministry projects, shared resource development, and accountability relationships — all of which are cooperative, not competitive. Competition would undermine the Core Values of Interdependence and Empowerment.
feedback.incorrect: The city group partnership is built on collaboration, not competition. The identified components are ongoing learning circles, collaborative ministry projects, shared resource development, and accountability relationships that sustain Breakthrough Plan implementation. Every element reinforces interdependence rather than rivalry.

variants.stem: Which of the following best describes the purpose of the city group partnership after Session 16?
options: Sustaining each person's Breakthrough Plan implementation through accountability and collaboration
Selecting which participant's Breakthrough Plan deserves the most organizational funding
Transitioning leadership of each participant's ministry to the city group as a whole
Completing a final group project required for DVULI certification

feedback.correct: The city group partnership exists to sustain Breakthrough Plan implementation through ongoing learning circles, collaborative ministry projects, shared resource development, and accountability relationships. It is a community of practice designed for mutual support and continued growth.
feedback.incorrect: The city group is not an evaluative or certifying body. Its purpose is ongoing mutual support — sustaining each person's Breakthrough Plan implementation through accountability relationships and collaborative work that continues the learning transfer beyond the formal program.

questions.stem: Classify each statement about the Breakthrough Plan presentation and Collaborative Leadership:

statements.text: A Breakthrough Plan must be strategic, significant, and sustainable to qualify as genuine breakthrough.
Peer feedback on a Breakthrough Plan may reveal synergies with other participants' plans.
Collaborative Leadership requires the leader to make all major decisions independently before sharing them.
The Collaborative Leadership skill of Assessing the Environment is needed after the DVULI program concludes.
A participant's Breakthrough Plan will need no modification after receiving peer feedback.
Sharing Power and Influence means abandoning your own vision in favor of the group's preferences.
The city group alumni community of practice can serve as an ongoing accountability structure for Breakthrough Plan implementation.

feedback.correct: The key distinctions here involve understanding what is definitionally required (the three-part Breakthrough definition is non-negotiable), what depends on context (synergies emerge based on plan overlap; some plans may already be strong), and what contradicts core principles (independent decision-making and vision abandonment both violate Collaborative Leadership).
feedback.incorrect: A common error is confusing 'Sharing Power' with giving up your vision, or assuming peer feedback always requires changes. Sharing Power means involving others in leadership while maintaining clarity of vision. And while feedback often improves plans, a well-developed plan might emerge from feedback affirmed rather than altered — making modification 'sometimes' rather than 'always' needed.

variants.stem: Classify each statement about the Session 16 structure and the DVULI capstone experience:

statements.text: Each Breakthrough Plan presentation includes both a participant's delivery and cohort feedback time.
The Breakthrough Plan Feedback Form asks peers to evaluate the plan's empowerment orientation.
A participant who demonstrates strong Self-Reflection will automatically excel at all other Collaborative Leadership skills.
City group members will identify collaborative ministry projects that span multiple Breakthrough Plans.
Nehemiah's leadership example is relevant only to construction and rebuilding projects.
The DVULI Personal Assessment Model dimensions of Calling, Character, and Commitment to Change are all addressed by the full program.
A Breakthrough Plan's sustainability depends solely on the individual leader's willpower and discipline.

feedback.correct: Understanding these distinctions requires grasping that Collaborative Leadership skills are independent competencies (strength in one doesn't guarantee others), that collaboration opportunities depend on plan alignment, and that sustainability is built through structures and accountability — not willpower alone.
feedback.incorrect: Key errors include thinking Self-Reflection automatically develops other skills (each must be cultivated independently), limiting Nehemiah's relevance to construction contexts (his principles apply broadly), or believing sustainability depends only on individual discipline (accountability structures and community are essential by design).

questions.stem: In the Breakthrough Plan presentation session, each participant receives a specific number of minutes for their presentation of Personal and Ministry Learning Journals, Focus Statements, and Breakthrough Plans. How many minutes does each participant have to present (not including feedback time)?

feedback.correct: Each participant receives exactly 15 minutes for their Breakthrough Plan presentation, followed by an additional 15 minutes of cohort feedback — totaling 30 minutes per participant. The equal time allocation embodies the Core Value of Balance.
feedback.incorrect: Students often overestimate presentation time, imagining a full seminar-length delivery. The structure is deliberately concise: 15 minutes for the presentation itself, which forces participants to distill their Learning Journals, Focus Statements, and Breakthrough Plans into a focused summary. An additional 15 minutes is allocated separately for peer feedback.

variants.stem: After each participant's Breakthrough Plan presentation, the cohort provides feedback. How many total minutes are allocated per participant for the combined presentation AND feedback cycle?

feedback.correct: Each participant receives 30 total minutes: 15 minutes for their presentation and 15 minutes for cohort feedback using the Breakthrough Plan Feedback Form. This equal allocation reflects the Core Value of Balance.
feedback.incorrect: The total is 30 minutes per participant — evenly split between 15 minutes of presentation and 15 minutes of feedback. Students sometimes underestimate by forgetting the feedback portion, or overestimate by assuming open-ended discussion time is added beyond the structured format.

questions.stem: Maria has completed her DVULI journey. Her Breakthrough Plan focuses on creating a youth mentoring network across three churches in her city. During her presentation, peers noted her plan clearly identifies leverage points but questioned whether her sustainability structures were strong enough. Her city group partner offered to co-lead a training component. As Maria processes this feedback and plans her next steps, which approach best reflects the integrated capstone learning of Session 16?
options: Strengthen her sustainability structures by building in city group accountability, accept the co-leadership offer as an expression of Interdependence, and use Self-Reflection to honestly assess where her plan needs refinement
Reject the co-leadership offer to maintain sole ownership of her vision, since Breakthrough Plans are personal documents
Delay implementation until she can independently resolve every weakness her peers identified
Focus exclusively on the leverage points her peers praised and minimize attention to the sustainability concerns
justifications: This approach integrates Breakthrough Plan criteria (sustainability), Core Values (Interdependence, Accountability), Collaborative Leadership skills (Self-Reflection, Sharing Power), and city group partnership — reflecting the full synthesis Session 16 demands
This approach is correct because accepting help from others is always the right thing to do in Christian ministry
This approach works because the Personal Assessment Model requires leaders to accept all feedback without critical evaluation
This approach is best because sustainability is the most important of the three Breakthrough criteria and should always take priority over strategy and significance

feedback.correct: Maria's best path forward synthesizes multiple Session 16 concepts: she uses Self-Reflection (a Collaborative Leadership skill) to honestly assess her plan's weaknesses, she accepts the co-leadership offer as an expression of Sharing Power and the Core Value of Interdependence, and she strengthens sustainability structures through city group accountability — the very mechanism Session 16's third movement establishes for ongoing implementation support.
feedback.incorrect: Rejecting collaboration contradicts both the Core Value of Interdependence and the Collaborative Leadership skill of Sharing Power. Delaying implementation until all problems are solved independently ignores the city group accountability structure designed to support ongoing refinement. Ignoring sustainability concerns would undermine one of the three non-negotiable criteria for Breakthrough (strategic, significant, sustainable).
feedback.wrongReason: Students may correctly choose the integrated approach but justify it with an oversimplified rationale — such as 'accepting help is always right' or 'sustainability is the most important criterion.' The real justification requires connecting specific concepts: Collaborative Leadership skills, Core Values, Breakthrough criteria, and the city group partnership structure all working together as Session 16 intends.

variants.stem: David presented his Breakthrough Plan to develop a community resource center for urban youth. His peers affirmed its strategic focus but noted that his plan relies heavily on his own effort without empowering others. One peer suggested a partnership between their two Breakthrough Plans to share training resources. David also realized during feedback that he had not adequately assessed the environment in his target neighborhood. What is David's best integrated response?
options: Redesign the plan's implementation to develop and empower local youth leaders, accept the cross-plan partnership, and conduct a thorough environmental assessment before launching
Keep the plan as-is since his peers affirmed its strategic focus, which is the most important Breakthrough criterion
Start over with an entirely new Breakthrough Plan that addresses every concern raised in feedback
Implement his original plan quickly before the feedback discourages him from acting
justifications: This integrates peer feedback (Accountability), empowerment orientation (a Breakthrough Plan evaluation criterion and Core Value), Collaborative Leadership skills (Assessing the Environment, Developing People, Sharing Power), and city group partnership — demonstrating the full synthesis of Session 16
This is correct because Breakthrough Leadership requires empowering others, and David's plan currently lacks this, so everything else must wait until empowerment is added
This approach works because the Personal Assessment Model's Commitment to Change dimension requires leaders to change everything whenever they receive critical feedback
This is best because the Collaborative Leadership skill of Building Trust requires accepting every suggestion from peers without question

feedback.correct: David's response integrates multiple concepts: the empowerment orientation that both the Core Values and Breakthrough Leadership definition demand, the Collaborative Leadership skills of Assessing the Environment and Developing People, the city group partnership as a resource-sharing mechanism, and the Accountability inherent in receiving and acting on honest peer feedback.
feedback.incorrect: Ignoring the empowerment gap contradicts the definition of Breakthrough Leadership (implementing a strategy to empower others). Starting over is unnecessary — the strategic foundation is sound. Rushing to implement before addressing feedback ignores the Accountability and Self-Reflection that Session 16 emphasizes.
feedback.wrongReason: Students may select the correct answer but justify it by elevating one concept above the others. The correct justification recognizes that multiple concepts — peer feedback, empowerment orientation, Collaborative Leadership skills, and city group partnership — must work together, not that any single concept drives the decision.

questions.stem: In Week 1, the Personal Assessment Model identified three dimensions that together produce Authenticity, Responsiveness, and Teachability. When revisited in Session 16, these three dimensions are:
options: Calling, Character, and Commitment to Change
Vision, Strategy, and Implementation
Knowledge, Skills, and Attitudes
Past Experience, Present Capacity, and Future Goals

feedback.correct: The DVULI Personal Assessment Model — Calling, Character, and Commitment to Change — was introduced in Session 1 and revisited in Session 16 to help participants see how the full program has developed each dimension. These three dimensions produce the qualities of Authenticity, Responsiveness, and Teachability.
feedback.incorrect: The Personal Assessment Model is specific to the DVULI curriculum: Calling, Character, and Commitment to Change. These are not generic leadership categories but a framework designed to capture the holistic development DVULI seeks — and Session 16 revisits them precisely to show how fifteen sessions have deepened each dimension.

variants.stem: The DVULI Personal Assessment Model, first introduced in Session 1 and revisited in the final session, identifies three dimensions of leader development. Which qualities do these three dimensions together produce?
options: Authenticity, Responsiveness, and Teachability
Courage, Competence, and Charisma
Vision, Passion, and Discipline
Humility, Integrity, and Resilience

feedback.correct: The three dimensions — Calling, Character, and Commitment to Change — together produce Authenticity, Responsiveness, and Teachability. Session 16 revisits this model to help participants reflect on how the full DVULI program has developed each dimension over fifteen sessions.
feedback.incorrect: While qualities like courage and integrity are valuable, the specific outcomes of the Personal Assessment Model are Authenticity (from Calling), Responsiveness (from Character), and Teachability (from Commitment to Change). These three qualities represent the holistic development DVULI seeks in its participants.

questions.stem: In Week 5, the Five Core Values were described as an interconnected system. In Session 16, all five values are simultaneously embodied in the peer-feedback activity. Which Core Value is specifically demonstrated when peers identify potential synergies between their own Breakthrough Plans and the presenter's plan?
options: Leverage
Accountability
Balance
Empowerment
justifications: Leverage is about identifying small actions with outsized Kingdom impact, and cross-plan synergies represent collaborative opportunities that multiply the effect of individual plans
Leverage applies here because it is the final Core Value listed and therefore covers anything not captured by the other four
This is Leverage because any time two people work together it automatically qualifies as leveraging resources
Leverage is correct because the feedback form specifically uses the word 'leverage' in its instructions

feedback.correct: Leverage — the principle that small, well-chosen actions can produce outsized Kingdom impact — is demonstrated when peers identify synergies across Breakthrough Plans. These collaborative opportunities multiply the effect of each individual plan, creating impact greater than the sum of its parts. This connects directly to the mustard seed principle from Session 8.
feedback.incorrect: While all five Core Values are present in the feedback activity, each manifests in a specific way. Identifying cross-plan synergies is specifically an expression of Leverage — finding collaborative opportunities that multiply impact. Accountability appears in honest feedback, Balance in equal time, Interdependence in community learning, and Empowerment in building capacity.
feedback.wrongReason: Students may correctly identify Leverage but justify it with a superficial reason — such as the word appearing on the form or any collaboration counting as leverage. The true justification connects to Leverage's deeper meaning: identifying strategic collaborative opportunities that multiply Kingdom impact beyond what individual efforts could achieve.

variants.stem: In Session 16's peer feedback process, each presenter receives equal time for their presentation and feedback. According to the Five Core Values system introduced in Week 5, which Core Value does this equal time allocation specifically embody?
options: Balance
Leverage
Accountability
Interdependence
justifications: Balance in the DVULI framework means ensuring wholeness and equity, and equal presentation time ensures every participant's plan receives the same level of attention and investment from the cohort
Balance applies here simply because it involves dividing time into equal portions, which is a mathematical balancing act
This is Balance because the other four Core Values are already assigned to other parts of the feedback activity
Balance is correct because giving everyone the same amount of time prevents conflict in the group

feedback.correct: Balance, as a Core Value, means ensuring wholeness and equity across the community. Equal presentation time ensures every participant's Breakthrough Plan receives the same level of cohort attention, investment, and feedback — no plan is privileged over another.
feedback.incorrect: Equal time allocation specifically embodies Balance — the Core Value concerned with wholeness and equity. While it may also support group harmony, the deeper reason is that Balance as a DVULI principle ensures equitable investment in every participant's development and plan.
feedback.wrongReason: Students may correctly name Balance but justify it as simple math or conflict prevention. The real justification connects to Balance as a principle of equitable investment: every person's Breakthrough Plan deserves the same level of community attention and feedback.

questions.stem: Evaluate the relationship between these two statements:
questions.assertion: Nehemiah's approach to rebuilding Jerusalem's walls exemplifies the resource-networking model studied in Session 15.
questions.reason: Nehemiah's model includes prayer, vision-casting, stakeholder understanding, specific asks, and gratitude — all elements that also appear in his demonstration of Collaborative Leadership skills in Session 16.

feedback.correct: Both statements are true, and the reason explains the assertion. Nehemiah serves as an exemplar in multiple sessions precisely because his leadership integrates resource networking (Session 15) with collaborative leadership (Session 16). His prayer, vision, stakeholder engagement, specific asks, and gratitude are simultaneously resource-networking strategies and expressions of Collaborative Leadership skills like Building Trust, Creating Clarity, and Assessing the Environment.
feedback.incorrect: Students sometimes think Nehemiah is used as an isolated example in each session. In fact, the curriculum intentionally returns to Nehemiah because his story integrates multiple DVULI competencies — resource networking and collaborative leadership are not separate in his example but deeply intertwined, which is why both statements are true and connected.

variants.assertion: Simon Sinek's Golden Circle (Why → How → What) from Session 15 is relevant to how participants present their Breakthrough Plans in Session 16.
variants.reason: Starting with 'Why' — the calling and purpose behind the plan — creates the kind of empathic connection and inspired partnership that makes a Breakthrough Plan presentation compelling to peers.

feedback.correct: Both statements are true, and the reason explains the assertion. The Golden Circle's inside-out communication model (Why → How → What) directly applies to Breakthrough Plan presentations: leading with calling and purpose — the 'Why' — inspires peers and creates the empathic connection that motivates genuine engagement, feedback, and potential collaboration.
feedback.incorrect: Some students may think the Golden Circle only applies to external fundraising or marketing contexts. However, communicating from the inside out — starting with purpose — is equally powerful when presenting a Breakthrough Plan to peers. The 'Why' connects to the presenter's Calling (from the Personal Assessment Model) and creates the conditions for meaningful feedback.

prompts.prompt: Honest answer: when someone gives you critical feedback, what's your first instinct — defend yourself, shut down, or actually take it in? Be real.
Which is harder — asking for help or giving up control? And which one holds leaders back more?
Think of the best leader you've ever been around. Did they earn your trust fast or did it take a long time? What did they do that made the difference?
Would you rather have a leader who's crystal clear about direction but makes all the decisions alone — OR one who shares power but things always feel a little uncertain?
Has this program actually changed something real about how you lead or how you see yourself? Or does most of that stuff fade once you're back in the grind?
If you could only keep ONE thing from everything you've learned this program — one idea, one habit, one shift — what would it be and why?

activities.title: Breakthrough Plan Lightning Panels
activities.description: The instructor divides the cohort into breakout rooms of 4–5 participants, with each room functioning as a peer review panel. Each person gets 3 minutes to share the core of their Breakthrough Plan — the problem, the proposed change, and their first 90-day action step. After each presentation, the group uses a structured feedback protocol: one thing that energizes them about the plan, one question they're genuinely curious about, and one 'brilliant friend' suggestion they'd offer. A designated timekeeper in each room keeps the pace tight. Rooms reconvene in the main session, where the instructor invites 2–3 volunteers to share the most energizing plan they heard — not their own — celebrating the cohort's collective vision. This surfaces diversity of context while creating mutual investment in each other's success.
activities.materials: Breakout rooms preconfigured in Zoom/Teams; optional shared feedback template in Google Docs or Miro sent to participants in advance
activities.learningGoal: Articulate and refine the Breakthrough Plan through peer accountability; practice giving and receiving developmental feedback
activities.scalingNotes: For 25 students, five breakout rooms of 5 works cleanly. If cohort is smaller (12–15), use rooms of 3–4 and extend sharing time to 4 minutes. For larger groups (30+), reduce feedback to two prompts per presenter to protect time.

activities.title: Nehemiah's Six Skills — Live Case Mapping
activities.description: The instructor assigns each of five breakout rooms one passage from Nehemiah (e.g., Neh. 1, 2:1–18, 4, 5, 6) and one primary question: 'Which of the six Collaborative Leadership skills is Nehemiah deploying here — and what does it look like in practice?'
