Each room has 8 minutes to discuss and fill in two boxes on a shared Miro board or Google Slide deck: the specific skill they see, and one quote or action from the text that proves it. The rooms then return to the main session for a rapid 'gallery debrief' — the instructor scrolls through each room's contribution live on screen, and the cohort calls out agreements, surprises, or disagreements in the chat. The instructor closes by asking: 'Which skill showed up least in Nehemiah — and what does that mean for your own leadership?' This generates lively debate and connects ancient text to immediate application.
activities.materials: Shared Miro board or Google Slides deck with one slide per breakout room, pre-loaded with the six skills and a text excerpt for each group; Bible access (online or app)
activities.learningGoal: Analyze Nehemiah as a concrete exemplar of all six Collaborative Leadership skills and connect those skills to real ministry contexts
activities.scalingNotes: Five rooms works perfectly for 25 students. With fewer than 20, collapse to three rooms and assign two passages each. The shared board is essential — it allows the debrief to be visual and communal rather than just a verbal report-out.

activities.title: The DVULI Growth Timeline — Then, Now, Next
activities.description: The instructor opens a shared Jamboard or Google Slide with three columns labeled 'Where I Began,' 'Where I Am Now,' and 'What I'm Carrying Forward,' with rows for each DVULI dimension: Calling, Character, and Commitment to Change. Each participant gets 5 minutes of quiet individual time to add sticky notes or typed entries to their row (identified by first name or a color). This is not a performance — it is a genuine personal audit. After individual writing, the instructor pairs participants in private chats or mini breakout rooms of 2 for 5 minutes to share one insight from their grid. The instructor then invites three volunteers to share one 'Now' or 'Next' insight with the full group, narrating what the program has genuinely shifted for them. The populated board becomes a visible artifact of the cohort's collective transformation and can be screenshot and shared as a closing keepsake.
activities.materials: Jamboard or Google Slides template with the 3x3 grid, shared with edit access before the session; colored sticky note options if using Jamboard
activities.learningGoal: Revisit the DVULI Personal Assessment Model as an integrative framework and name concrete growth across Calling, Character, and Commitment to Change
activities.scalingNotes: This works for any size up to 40 with no modification — the async individual writing phase means the board scales naturally. For very large groups, the pair-share protects intimacy even when the main-room debrief is brief.

activities.title: City Group Forward Pact
activities.description: City groups (existing sub-cohort clusters) are sent to breakout rooms with a single shared Google Doc pre-loaded with three fill-in-the-blank prompts: (1) 'One ministry collaboration we will actually attempt together in the next 6 months is…'; (2) 'We will check in on each other's Breakthrough Plans by… [date/method]'; and (3) 'The way we stay connected as alumni is…' Groups have 10 minutes to discuss and complete their pact — not a wish list, but specific, named commitments with real dates and people. Each group selects one spokesperson to share their boldest commitment with the full cohort when rooms close. The instructor collects all docs and emails them back to each city group after class as a living accountability artifact. This activity bridges the program experience to sustainable ongoing practice and honors the relational investment already made.
activities.materials: City group breakout rooms pre-configured; shared Google Doc template per group, with edit links distributed before session; instructor collects docs post-session for follow-up email
activities.learningGoal: Activate the City Group partnership model as a vehicle for ongoing learning transfer, ministry collaboration, and alumni community of practice
activities.scalingNotes: Works directly for 25-person cohort with 4–6 city groups of 4–6 each. If city groups are very small (2–3 people), prompt them to be even more specific and personal in their commitments. If someone is without a city group, the instructor forms a temporary 'wildcard' group.

activities.title: The Six Skills Hot Seat — Real Scenarios, Real Debate
activities.description: The instructor presents three short, realistic ministry leadership scenarios in rapid succession via screen share — one at a time — each describing a tension a leader is facing (e.g., a team member losing motivation, a community refusing to trust a new initiative, a leader hoarding decisions). After each scenario, participants have 60 seconds to type into the chat: which of the six Collaborative Leadership skills is most critical right now, and one sentence of reasoning. The instructor then selects two or three responses that disagree with each other and names the tension live: 'We have Trust-Building over here and Creating Clarity over there — who wants to make the case for their choice?' This sparks genuine debate about how the skills interact, which ones get overused, and which tend to be neglected under pressure. The instructor closes by asking: 'Which of the six is your personal growing edge?' — inviting honest self-disclosure in the chat before the session ends.
activities.materials: Three ministry scenario slides prepared in advance and screen-shared; chat function active; no breakout rooms needed — this is a full-group activity
activities.learningGoal: Apply the six Collaborative Leadership skills to ambiguous real-world scenarios and identify personal development edges
activities.scalingNotes: The chat-based response format works for any size — 15 or 150. For larger groups, the instructor may need to be more selective about which chat responses to elevate. For very small groups (under 12), skip the chat and do a verbal round-robin instead.

activities.title: Commissioning Statements — The Leader I Am Becoming
activities.description: This is the closing ritual of the entire program. The instructor unmutes everyone or opens a final round-robin, asking each participant to complete one sentence aloud — no longer than 15 seconds each: 'The leader I am becoming is someone who…' Participants are not given prep time beyond 60 seconds of silent reflection, which keeps statements raw and genuine rather than rehearsed. The instructor or a co-facilitator types each statement live into a shared doc or chat as participants speak, creating a running document of the cohort's collective identity. After everyone has spoken, the instructor reads back three or four statements that represent the range and depth of the group's commitments, then offers a brief commissioning blessing or charge before closing. This creates a memorable, emotionally resonant ending that honors the full arc of the program and sends participants forward with communal witness to their commitments.
activities.materials: Shared Google Doc or visible chat for capturing statements; instructor may optionally prepare a brief 2-minute closing charge or reading; no tech required beyond basic audio
activities.learningGoal: Synthesize the full program journey into a public identity statement; create shared accountability and communal commissioning as the cohort transitions to alumni status
activities.scalingNotes: For 25 people at 15 seconds each, this runs approximately 6–7 minutes of statements plus 3–4 minutes of instructor reflection — tight but doable. For groups over 35, use the chat as the primary medium (everyone types their statement simultaneously) and the instructor reads select statements aloud to preserve the ritual quality.

slides.title: Accelerating Change
slides.bodyText: You've spent this entire program becoming a different kind of leader — today, you prove it.
slides.speakerNotes: Welcome students warmly and with weight. This is the final session — acknowledge that. Tell them: 'Every framework, every hard conversation, every journal entry has been pointing here. Today is not the end of DVULI. It is the beginning of what DVULI actually produces.' Preview the arc: we will present Breakthrough Plans, revisit the six skills of collaborative leadership through Nehemiah, and look honestly at who you've become through the Calling, Character, and Commitment to Change model.

slides.title: Your Breakthrough Plan
slides.bodyText: The culminating event where learning becomes action and peers become your accountability partners.
slides.speakerNotes: Transition from the opening by saying something like: 'We have talked about problems, frameworks, and theories. Now we talk about YOUR city, YOUR ministry, YOUR next move.' Set the tone — this is not a class presentation. It is a public commitment. The room is the witness.

slides.title: What Makes a Breakthrough Plan
bullets: A real, specific challenge rooted in your ministry context
Concrete steps grounded in the program's frameworks
Measurable outcomes you can track over time
An accountability structure designed to outlast this room
slides.speakerNotes: Walk through each component. Emphasize 'real and specific' — vague plans produce no change. Ask: 'Is your challenge one your city group can actually hold you to six months from now?' Push back gently on plans that are too spiritual-sounding and not concrete enough. Give an example: not 'improve trust in my organization' but 'hold monthly listening sessions with frontline staff starting in February, with a written summary shared to all.'

slides.title: Why Presentation Matters
slides.bodyText: Speaking your plan aloud — to people who have watched you grow — is the moment a vision becomes a commitment.
slides.speakerNotes: Pause here. Really let this land. You might say: 'There is neuroscience behind this. When we articulate a plan publicly, our brains encode it differently than a private journal entry. You are not presenting for a grade. You are making a vow.' Ask the room: 'Who is someone — outside this cohort — who needs to hear your Breakthrough Plan this week?' Let a few people respond.

slides.title: Giving and Receiving Peer Feedback
bullets: Lead with specific strengths before naming gaps
Ask clarifying questions — don't steer with loaded ones
Offer one concrete, actionable challenge per presenter
Receive feedback with curiosity, not self-defense
slides.speakerNotes: Model this before presentations begin. Role-play a weak feedback response ('That was great, very inspiring') versus a strong one ('I noticed your plan doesn't name who owns each step — what would happen if you assigned initials to each action?'). Remind the group: good feedback is a form of trust. It says, 'I believe you can handle the truth because I believe in where you're going.'

slides.title: Collaborative Leadership
slides.bodyText: Six skills that separate positional leaders from transformational ones — and a 2,500-year-old leader who practiced every single one.
slides.speakerNotes: Transition by saying: 'Before we look forward, let's name what kind of leader DVULI has been trying to build. These six skills are not a checklist — they are a posture. And no one modeled this posture better than a man who rebuilt a city in 52 days.' Build anticipation for Nehemiah before you name the skills.

slides.title: The Six Skills of Collaborative Leaders
bullets: Self-Reflection — know your shadow before it leads
Trust-Building — earned slowly, lost instantly
Developing People — your legacy is who they become
Assessing Environment — read the room and the city
Creating Clarity — ambiguity is the enemy of action
Sharing Power — abundance over scarcity
slides.speakerNotes: Don't rush through this list. For each skill, pause and ask students to rate themselves 1–5 silently. Which one is their edge? Which one do they avoid? After the list, say: 'Nehemiah didn't read a leadership book. But watch how naturally these six skills appear in his story.' This sets up the quote and two-column slides that follow.

slides.title: Nehemiah 2:17
slides.bodyText: "Come, let us rebuild the wall of Jerusalem, and we will no longer be in disgrace." He didn't say 'I will build.' He said 'let us.'
slides.speakerNotes: Read this slowly. Then say: 'Two words. Let us. That is the entire philosophy of collaborative leadership in two words.' Nehemiah was the king's cupbearer — he had access to power. He could have issued orders. Instead he invited participation, named a shared problem, and created a shared vision. Ask: 'When was the last time you changed an 'I will' plan into a 'let us' invitation?' Give students 60 seconds to write a response.

slides.title: Nehemiah and the Six Skills
bullets: Prayed and fasted before acting
Inspected the wall secretly by night
Named the shared disgrace plainly
Assigned sections by household and guild
slides.speakerNotes: Walk this comparison left to right, row by row. Emphasize that Nehemiah never skipped steps. He reflected before he spoke, surveyed before he planned, named the problem before he recruited, and then distributed ownership rather than centralizing it. Ask: 'Which of these four moves do you most often skip in your own leadership?' Notice patterns in the room — most leaders skip either self-reflection or sharing power.

slides.title: Who You've Become
slides.bodyText: Revisiting the DVULI Personal Assessment Model — not as a starting point, but as a mirror of everything this program has built in you.
slides.speakerNotes: This section shift should feel different — more reflective, slower. Say: 'We are going to change gears. Put down your pen for a moment. We started this program by looking at three dimensions of your leadership identity. Let's see what's changed.' The tone here is honoring and contemplative, not instructional.

slides.title: The DVULI Personal Assessment Model
bullets: Calling — the 'why' beneath everything you lead
Character — who you are when no one is watching
Commitment to Change — your willingness to be disrupted
slides.speakerNotes: For each dimension, ask a reflective question and allow brief sharing. For Calling: 'Has your sense of calling been sharpened, challenged, or expanded this year?' For Character: 'Name one character gap this program exposed in you.' For Commitment to Change: 'What is the hardest thing you've had to unlearn?' Don't rush. These are the most important questions of the session.

slides.title: The Deepest Truth
slides.bodyText: You cannot lead transformation in your city if you refuse transformation in yourself.
slides.speakerNotes: Let this sit in silence for five full seconds before you speak. Then say: 'This is the thesis of DVULI. Not a technique. Not a framework. A way of being.' You might invite students to write this statement somewhere they will see it weekly — a phone wallpaper, a journal cover, a sticky note in the office. Ask: 'Which of the three dimensions — Calling, Character, or Commitment to Change — has grown the most in you this year? Tell someone next to you.'

slides.title: City Group Partnership & Alumni Community
bullets: City groups: sustained peer learning beyond this cohort
Cross-organizational ministry collaboration in your city
Alumni community of practice: shared frameworks, real challenges
You are now a resource for leaders who come after you
slides.speakerNotes: Shift the energy forward now. Ask: 'Who in this room do you want to stay in relationship with?' Then explain that city groups are not optional social extras — they are the primary mechanism for making sure what you learned here actually changes your city. Describe the alumni network as an ongoing peer consultation community. Challenge students to schedule their first post-program city group gathering before they leave today.

slides.title: On What Communities of Practice Actually Do
slides.bodyText: "We do not learn from experience. We learn from reflecting on experience." — John Dewey. Your alumni community is where reflection stays alive.
slides.speakerNotes: Use Dewey's insight to explain why the alumni network matters beyond networking. Say: 'Without a community to reflect with, experience becomes habit, not wisdom. Your city group is how you keep learning from everything that is about to happen.' Ask: 'What is one question you want your city group to hold you accountable to over the next year?' Let two or three people share aloud.

slides.title: Key Takeaways
bullets: Your Breakthrough Plan is a living commitment — not a final assignment
Collaborative leaders share power, build trust, and never stop growing
Calling, Character, and Commitment to Change are lifelong disciplines
Your city group and alumni network are your ongoing accountability
What comes next begins the moment you walk out of this room
slides.speakerNotes: Close with energy and warmth. Read each takeaway slowly, letting each one land. Then say: 'DVULI did not make you a leader. You were already one. DVULI gave you language, community, and a mirror. What you do with what you see is entirely up to you.' Invite a moment of prayer or silent reflection, then send them out with your blessing. This cohort is now part of something larger than themselves.

blocks.title: Audiobook
blocks.transcript:
Picture this: It is the morning of Local Workshop Five. The room holds the same eleven people who sat together nervously on the first night of this program fifteen months ago, introducing themselves, unsure what the journey ahead would ask of them. But something is different now. The nervousness that fills the room today is not the nervousness of strangers — it is the holy, trembling anticipation of people who have done real work on themselves, on their ministries, and on each other. Each person holds a document. Not a homework assignment. Not a workbook exercise. A BREAKTHROUGH PLAN — their own carefully written account of who they are, what God has called them to build, and precisely how they intend to build it.

One by one they will stand before the cohort. They will be seen — perhaps more fully than at any previous moment in the program. They will receive honest, loving feedback from peers who now know them well enough to tell the truth. And in the hours that follow the presentations, they will commit — publicly, accountably, and within the community of practice that has sustained them through this journey — to do the specific, courageous, trajectory-altering work to which God has called them. This session is not the end of the program. It is the moment the program has been preparing them for.

[short pause]

Fifteen sessions of intentional learning have been building toward this room, this morning, this moment. Session One introduced the Personal Assessment Model — the hexagonal framework of Calling, Character, Commitment to Change, Authenticity, Responsiveness, and Teachability — as a diagnostic for understanding one's divine design. Every subsequent workshop and national conference added a layer of insight, skill, and relational depth. Community Youth Development challenged the cohort to see their neighborhoods through an asset lens rather than a deficit lens. Scenario Planning trained them to imagine multiple futures and act with strategic flexibility. Systems Thinking revealed the hidden structures beneath the surface events of ministry life. Resource Networking equipped them to mobilize people, goods, and services for Kingdom purposes. And through every session, the five Core Values — Accountability, Balance, Interdependence, Empowerment, and Leverage — have served as the ethical and relational spine of all of it.

The Breakthrough Plan that each participant brings to this final session is the integrating artifact of that entire journey. According to the DVULI Breakthrough Handbook, Breakthrough is defined as "strategic, significant, and sustainable change in the way you live and work," and Breakthrough Leadership as "implementing a strategy to empower others for ministry with urban youth." These definitions, first introduced at the First National Conference's capstone session on Leverage and Breakthrough, now carry the full weight of fifteen months of learning, reflection, relationship, and courageous self-examination behind them. The plan is not a theoretical document. It is a covenant with a specific future that God has called each leader to help create.

[short pause]

Research on the transfer of training illuminates why this session matters so much. Baldwin and Ford's landmark review of the training transfer literature, published in 1988, found that fewer than ten percent of training investment typically transfers to sustained job performance without deliberate environmental reinforcement, supervisor support, and peer accountability. DVULI's design counters this with extraordinary intentionality: the Breakthrough Plan presentation creates a moment of public commitment, the cohort feedback structures ongoing peer accountability, and the alumni community of practice provides the relational environment in which the plan can actually live and breathe over time.

Before moving on, pause and consider: of the fifteen months you have invested in this program, which single insight or experience do you expect to be most central to your Breakthrough Plan? What made it so formative — and what structural conditions will you need to put in place to ensure it does not fade?

[short pause]

Now let's look at how this session is structured. It unfolds in three movements, and the first — the most emotionally and spiritually significant — is the Breakthrough Plan presentation sequence. Each participant presents a fifteen-minute oral summary of their Personal and Ministry Learning Journals, Focus Statements, and Breakthrough Plans, followed by fifteen minutes of structured cohort feedback. The thirty-minute cycle per presenter is carefully designed: the time constraint forces presenters to distill their learning to its essential core, and the feedback structure channels the cohort's responses toward specific, constructive, and forward-looking input.

The presentation itself invites each leader to do something that is both intellectually demanding and personally vulnerable: to narrate their own TRANSFORMATION. Effective presenters do not simply read their plan. They tell the story of what they discovered about themselves, what shifted in their understanding of their ministry, and what specific, high-leverage changes they are now committed to implementing. They are practicing what Goleman, Boyatzis, and McKee described in their 2013 work on primal leadership as the foundational competency of resonant leadership — the capacity for self-awareness and self-disclosure that creates genuine connection with others and invites authentic collaboration. The presentation is an act of leadership, not just a reporting event.

[short pause]

The Breakthrough Plan Feedback Form is a sophisticated pedagogical tool that structures cohort responses along five dimensions. Peers are invited to assess which of the five Core Values are clearly evident in the presenter's plan; which Breakthrough Skills — Community Youth Development, Scenario Planning, Systems Thinking, Resource Networking, and Collaboration — are most prominently reflected in the strategy; and to evaluate the plan's orientation toward empowering others for ministry with urban youth rather than consolidating ministry activity around the leader themselves. Two additional dimensions complete the process: peers suggest specific strategies for overcoming the presenter's identified barriers, and they identify potential synergies between the presenter's plan and their own — pointing toward the collaborative ministry possibilities that will animate the third movement of this session.

This feedback architecture is grounded in what Boud, Cohen, and Sampson described in their 2001 work on peer learning as the core advantage of structured peer learning: when learners move from passive recipients to active co-constructors of each other's knowledge, both the quality of learning and the depth of accountability increase dramatically.

Perhaps most importantly, the feedback process embodies all five Core Values simultaneously. The honest appraisal of a peer's plan is an act of Accountability. The equal time allocation for each presenter reflects Balance. The cohort functioning as a learning community is the very definition of Interdependence. Building each participant's capacity to lead is Empowerment. And identifying synergies across plans for potential collaboration is the essence of Leverage. The feedback session is not an administrative requirement appended to the presentations — it is a live demonstration of everything the program has been teaching.

[short pause]

The second movement of this session introduces COLLABORATIVE LEADERSHIP — defined in the curriculum as "the capacity to bring peers together, and enable them to look beyond their own talent and resources to accomplish a shared goal." The collaborative leader inspires commitment and action in others and uses their influence to keep diverse stakeholders engaged with a shared vision. This is the capstone competency of the program, because it is only through collaborative leadership that Breakthrough Plans achieve their full potential impact.

The curriculum identifies six specific skills of collaborative leadership, drawn from the Turning Point Collaborative Leadership framework. Together, these skills map almost perfectly onto the arc of learning across all fifteen prior sessions. The first is Self-Reflection — understanding one's own values and ensuring behaviors are congruent with them. This was the work of Session One, the First National Conference on Core Values, and the Anatomy of Peace sessions. The second is Building Trust — creating the relational conditions in which diverse stakeholders can share ideas honestly and remain committed through difficulty. This was developed through the communication tools of Local Workshop One, the mentoring relationships, and the cohort's group dialogues.

The third skill is Developing People — the Empowerment Core Value in action: mentoring, coaching, and releasing others into leadership. The fourth is Assessing the Environment — reading the systems, scenarios, and stakeholders accurately enough to identify common ground and plan for obstacles. Local Workshop Three's Scenario Planning content, the Second National Conference's Systems Thinking work, and the community asset mapping exercises all built this capacity. The fifth skill is Creating Clarity and Vision — articulating a compelling, shared vision that transcends individual self-interest. The Breakthrough Plan presentations themselves are an exercise in this skill. And the sixth is Sharing Power and Influence — structuring decision-making processes that draw on the expertise and ownership of everyone at the table, not just the designated leader.

[short pause]

Kouzes and Posner argue across decades of research — most recently in their 2017 edition of The Leadership Challenge — that "Enable Others to Act" — the practice most closely aligned with Sharing Power and Influence — is consistently among the most impactful of the five exemplary leadership practices, yet also among the most counterintuitive for leaders who have been rewarded throughout their careers for individual expertise and decisive action. DVULI's curriculum recognizes this tension and engages it directly through every session. The leader who graduates from this program should be measurably more comfortable with distributed leadership, shared authority, and collective ownership of outcomes than the leader who enrolled fifteen months ago.

Take a moment to reflect on this: of the six collaborative leadership skills, which feels most natural to you after this program — and which still represents your growing edge? What specific experience in DVULI most challenged your default approach to that skill? How does your Breakthrough Plan address it?

[short pause]

The curriculum has used Nehemiah as a leadership case study across multiple sessions, but this final session positions him as the definitive biblical model of collaborative leadership — the leader who embodies all six skills in a single, sustained ministry project. Gary Smith's 2010 commentary on Ezra-Nehemiah traces the full arc of Nehemiah's leadership method with exegetical precision, and the pattern is unmistakable.

Nehemiah's environmental assessment — the famous nighttime circuit of Jerusalem's broken walls, conducted privately before he disclosed his vision to a single stakeholder — is a masterclass in reading the system before attempting to change it. His transparent communication of vision, in which he first names the problem honestly — "You see the trouble we are in" — then articulates a divinely-sourced possibility, and only then invites shared commitment — "Let us rebuild" — maps precisely onto the skill of Creating Clarity and Vision. His coalition-building across tribal, occupational, and gender lines, which includes at least one group of daughters working alongside their father, is a living illustration of sharing power and influence across every available boundary.

Heifetz and Linsky draw on Nehemiah extensively in their 2002 framework for adaptive leadership, noting that the wall-rebuilding project is not a technical problem — the techniques of masonry were well known — but an ADAPTIVE CHALLENGE requiring the community of Jerusalem to change how they understood themselves, their capacity for self-governance, and their relationship to the broken covenant with God. Nehemiah's response to opposition demonstrates what Heifetz and Linsky call "holding steady under pressure" — refusing to be drawn off the balcony and into the fray at a level that would disrupt the larger process. The accountability structures for completion, including the registration and reestablishment of community life after the wall was finished, model the sustainability dimension of Breakthrough that the curriculum insists upon.

[short pause]

The third movement of this session turns the cohort's gaze forward — not to a conclusion, but to a continuation. Etienne Wenger's foundational 1998 theory of communities of practice provides the conceptual architecture for understanding why this matters. Wenger argues that learning is not primarily an individual cognitive event but a fundamentally social one: it is "situated in participation within communities" where "shared practice, mutual engagement, and joint enterprise create sustained professional identity and knowledge development." The DVULI city group, over fifteen months, has become precisely such a community. The question is what kind of intentionality is required to sustain it after the formal program ends.

The curriculum identifies four primary ways the city group can continue to function as a productive community of practice. The first is ongoing learning circles — regular gatherings, meeting every six to eight weeks, in which group members share what they are encountering in their Breakthrough Plan implementation, raise new challenges for group dialogue, and continue the practices of giving and receiving honest feedback that have characterized the program. Pedler and Abbott, writing in 2013 on action learning, describe this as "working on real problems with real stakes" in "small groups that provide challenge and support simultaneously." The learning circle is not a social gathering with a spiritual veneer — it is a structured accountability and growth environment.

The second modality is collaborative ministry projects — specific initiatives that emerge from the synergies identified in the Breakthrough Plan feedback process. When multiple participants in a single city group are working on related aspects of youth development — one focused on leadership pipeline development, another on community asset mapping, a third on empowerment-oriented discipleship — the natural collaboration potential is significant. The third is shared resource development — leveraging the collective expertise, networks, and platform of the entire cohort to access resources that no single ministry could attract alone. Local Workshop Four's Resource Networking content equipped participants with the tools to articulate their "Why" powerfully and to identify partners across multiple resource circles. The city group, functioning as a unified community of practice, can bring these skills to bear on behalf of all its members' Breakthrough Plans collectively.

The fourth modality — and most fundamental — is the accountability relationships that will sustain each person's Breakthrough Plan implementation in the months ahead. Each graduate is expected to identify an accountability partner, check in monthly, and report on both progress and unexpected barriers. This structure embodies the Accountability Core Value at its most specific and practical: as the First National Conference Workbook describes it, "honest introspection, the prayerful search for wisdom, and the development of accountability relationships characterized by a mutual commitment to seek and tell the truth concerning each other's thoughts and actions."

There is an African proverb worth carrying with you: "If you want to go fast, go alone. If you want to go far, go together."

[short pause]

This session also closes the loop on the journey that began in Session One with the most important act of self-examination the program asks of its graduates: a full-arc revisitation of the Personal Assessment Model. The six-dimensional framework — Calling, Character, Commitment to Change, Authenticity, Responsiveness, and Teachability — was introduced in Local Workshop One as a diagnostic tool. Now, at the program's end, it serves as a mirror in which each graduate can see both the distance they have traveled and the direction that remains ahead.

These six dimensions carry precise definitions. CALLING asks whether the graduate is "faithfully doing the work that God has called and equipped them to do as their contribution to building His Kingdom" — and whether the Breakthrough Plan now aligns more fully with that sense of calling than when they entered the program. Character asks whether a person is "consistently living out the values they espouse, applying them to every action and decision." The fifteen months of Core Values work should have produced measurable growth in the congruence between espoused values and lived behavior.

Commitment to Change asks whether a leader is "willingly adjusting their thoughts, plans and actions to align them more fully with God's will." This is perhaps the dimension the program tests most rigorously. The adaptive challenges Heifetz and Linsky describe — the genuine losses, the identity disruptions, the necessary letting go of familiar patterns — are precisely the crucible in which Commitment to Change is forged or found wanting. Authenticity, which sits at the intersection of Calling and Character, is defined as "integrating both doing what God calls me to do and being who God has designed me to be" — the evidence that internal work and external work have begun to converge. Responsiveness, at the intersection of Calling and Commitment to Change, is "having the attitude of Christ — being willing to submit my will to God's will, as modeled by Jesus in his responsiveness to people, circumstances, and most importantly, His Father's will." And Teachability, at the intersection of Character and Commitment to Change, is "wanting to be transformed through the renewing of my mind — in essence, being a learner rather than a knower." This is the disposition that made fifteen months of stretching, confronting, and transformative learning possible — and it is the disposition that will determine whether the next fifteen months produce further growth or gradual reversion to familiar patterns.

[short pause]

The completed hexagon assessment — overlaid with the Session One baseline and annotated with three reflective paragraphs per dimension, addressing what shifted, what specific experience developed it, and what that growth means for the Breakthrough Plan — is compiled into what the program calls the DIVINE DESIGN PORTRAIT. This one-page artifact is both a graduation document and an accountability anchor: a precise, personal record of the leader this graduate has become, and a baseline against which continued growth can be measured in the years ahead. It is, in the most literal sense, a portrait of divine design — the unique work God has called and equipped this specific person to do, in the place He has designed for them, for the purposes He most needs accomplished in the world.

[short pause]

This session closes with the conviction that has been the north star of this program from the very first session. The biblical foundation of Local Workshop One frames the entire journey with Paul's words from Galatians Six and Romans Twelve: "be transformed by the renewing of your mind... Do not think of yourself more highly than you ought, but rather think of yourself with sober judgment, in accordance with the faith God has distributed to each of you." The journey that began with that honest, sometimes painful, always generative self-examination reaches its culmination not in a certificate or a celebration, but in a commitment: to do the unique work God has called and equipped each leader to do, in the place He has designed for them, for the purposes He most needs accomplished in the world.

That commitment is what the Breakthrough Plan represents at its most fundamental level. It is not a strategic document in the conventional organizational sense. It is a statement of identity, vocation, and accountability — a public covenant that says: I know who I am. I know what I am called to build. I know how I will do it, with whom I will do it, and what I will do when the inevitable barriers arise. I know that I cannot do it alone, and I have built the community of accountability and collaboration that will hold me to it. And I know that the measure of my success is not what I accomplish by myself, but how many others I have equipped, released, and empowered to lead alongside me.

[short pause]

Let's take a moment to consolidate the key ideas from this session. First: this session is a launch, not a conclusion — the Breakthrough Plan presentations mark the beginning of implementation, not the end of learning. Second: the peer feedback process is a living enactment of all five Core Values simultaneously — Accountability, Balance, Interdependence, Empowerment, and Leverage. Third: Collaborative Leadership, the six-skill capstone competency, integrates every prior session's learning into an ongoing posture of empowering others for ministry. Fourth: Nehemiah is the definitive biblical model of collaborative leadership — he assessed the environment, built trust, communicated vision transparently, shared power across every available boundary, and built accountability structures to sustain the work. Fifth: the city group's ongoing community of practice — through learning circles, collaborative projects, shared resource development, and accountability relationships — is the structural mechanism that ensures Breakthrough Plan implementation does not fade, as Baldwin and Ford's 1988 research on training transfer reminds us it so easily can. Sixth: the revisited Personal Assessment Model, annotated with reflective paragraphs, becomes the Divine Design Portrait — a precise record of the leader this graduate has become, and an anchor for continued growth. And seventh: Breakthrough is "strategic, significant, and sustainable change in the way you live and work." Breakthrough Leadership is "implementing a strategy to empower others for ministry with urban youth." These definitions now carry fifteen months of lived experience behind them.

[short pause]

The graduate's journey continues through monthly accountability check-ins, ongoing city group learning circles, collaboration grant applications for joint ministry projects, and the alumni network that connects DVULI graduates across more than twenty-five years of cohorts. The question the program leaves with each graduate is the same one with which it began: What is the unique work God has called and equipped you to do, in the place He has designed for you, for the purposes He most needs accomplished in the world? The answer has been coming into focus across fifteen months. Now it is time to live it.

blocks.title: Infographic
blocks.prompt: Create a 16:9 landscape educational infographic on a warm off-white background (#faf8f5) titled "From Foundation to Breakthrough: Your DVULI Leadership Journey" in bold dark navy (#00558C) at the top center. The infographic uses a visual metaphor of three converging pathways leading to a central launchpad moment. On the LEFT THIRD, display the DVULI Personal Assessment Model as three stacked rounded rectangles with icons: a compass icon labeled "CALLING" in navy (#00558C), a shield icon labeled "CHARACTER" in green (#319B41), and a flame icon labeled "COMMITMENT TO CHANGE" in warm amber (#d97706). A subtle dotted curved arrow connects each rectangle upward, showing progressive development. In the CENTER THIRD, display a hexagonal cluster of six hexagons arranged in a honeycomb pattern, with the header "6 Collaborative Leadership Skills" in green (#319B41). Each hexagon has a small icon and short label: "Self-Reflection" (mirror icon), "Trust-Building" (handshake icon), "Developing People" (seedling icon), "Assessing Environment" (radar icon), "Creating Clarity" (lightbulb icon), and "Sharing Power" (open hands icon). All hexagons have thin navy borders with white fills. Below the honeycomb, a small banner reads "Nehemiah: The Exemplar" in italic navy text with a subtle scroll icon. Flowing arrows in a gradient from navy to green connect the left assessment model into the center hexagon cluster, showing how Calling, Character, and Commitment feed into the six skills. On the RIGHT THIRD, illustrate the culmination: a large upward-pointing chevron or launchpad shape in a gradient from navy (#00558C) to green (#319B41) labeled "BREAKTHROUGH PLAN" in bold white text inside it. Below the chevron, three horizontal pill-shaped badges in lighter tints show the ongoing elements: "Peer Feedback & Accountability" with a speech bubble icon, "City Group Partnership" with a connected nodes icon, and "Alumni Community of Practice" with a circular arrows icon. Thin elegant arrows flow from the center hexagons into the Breakthrough Plan chevron. At the very bottom, a slim horizontal bar spans the width reading "The End of the Program — The Beginning of What Comes Next" in small caps navy text. Use clean sans-serif typography throughout, maintain generous white space between sections, apply subtle drop shadows on cards and hexagons, and use the color palette consistently: primary navy #00558C for structure, green #319B41 for growth elements, warm amber #d97706 for emphasis accents, with light gray (#e6edf5) for subtle background shapes behind each section.

config.concept: Breakthrough Plan peer feedback as embodiment of all five Core Values
config.description: An interactive feedback form structured around the Breakthrough Plan Feedback Form from the DVULI curriculum. Reviewers click checkboxes for which Core Values and Breakthrough Skills are clearly evident in the presenter's plan, then use slider scales (1–5) to rate four dimensions: Clarity of personal and ministry focus, Strength of the empowerment strategy, Realism of the action steps and timeline, and Alignment between personal breakthrough and ministry breakthrough. Two open text fields collect: (1) the one suggestion most likely to help the presenter overcome their identified barriers and (2) the most natural collaboration opportunity the reviewer sees between this plan and their own. The tool aggregates feedback from all cohort members into a summary radar chart the presenter receives at the end of the session.
config.rationale: The aggregated radar chart delivers collective wisdom in a form that is immediately actionable — the presenter can see at a glance which dimensions of their plan the full cohort rates as strongest and which most need development, without being overwhelmed by 24 individual feedback documents.

config.concept: Collaborative Leadership six skills as the capstone competency of the DVULI program
config.description: A self-assessment widget where participants rate themselves on each of the six Collaborative Leadership skills at three time points: 'Before DVULI (beginning rating),' 'Now (end-of-program rating),' and 'My 12-Month Goal.' A growth trajectory visualization displays all three points on a single axis for each skill, showing both the distance traveled and the direction of continued development. Participants click any skill to see which specific DVULI sessions most developed that skill, which Core Value most closely aligns with it, and one concrete next action for continuing growth. A printable Collaborative Leadership Development Plan summarizes the participant's goals and next steps for the year ahead.
config.rationale: Displaying growth from 'before' to 'now' alongside '12-month goals' on a single timeline creates a metacognitive portrait of the participant's development journey — they can see not just where they are but how far they have come and what direction they are moving, generating both gratitude and purposeful forward motivation.

config.concept: Personal Assessment Model: Calling, Character, and Commitment to Change revisited at program's end
config.description: A return to the Session 1 Personal Assessment Model Hexagon Explorer, now populated with the participant's end-of-program self-assessments on all six dimensions (Calling, Character, Commitment to Change, Authenticity, Responsiveness, Teachability). The widget overlays the Session 1 assessment scores (retrieved from the saved Session 1 widget data) and the current scores on the same hexagon, shading the area of growth in green and any areas of decline in amber. Participants click each node to write a one-paragraph learning reflection: 'What shifted in my understanding of this dimension?' 'What specific experience in this program most developed it?' 'What does this growth mean for my Breakthrough Plan?' The completed hexagon and reflections are compiled into a one-page 'Divine Design Portrait' that serves as both a graduation artifact and an accountability anchor.
config.rationale: Returning to the exact assessment instrument from Session 1 — with the original scores retrieved and displayed alongside current scores — creates the most powerful possible spacing connection in the program: participants experience their own growth by comparing the person they were in Session 1 with the person they are in Session 16, encoding their transformation through direct, visual contrast.
